1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron, saying," The very brief verse notes the LORD speaking concerning the law of leprosy. The grammatical voices note the introduction. Henry and Gill note that "the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron, saying" — the LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron, now to give the long law of leprosy (the plague in man, ch. 13, and the cleansing, ch. 14); the speaking (God's command, the law of leprosy); the to Moses and Aaron (both the lawgiver and the high priest, for the priests were to judge the plague); the occasion (after the laws of purification, now the law of leprosy, the great figure of sin and its defilement).

On "the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron," the reformed and witnesses note the word. Gill and Ellicott note the LORD speaking to Moses and Aaron (the priests to judge the leprosy, the law given to both) — the new law (the law of leprosy, the priests' office of discernment).

On the speaking, the grammatical-historical note the command: the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron — the law of leprosy introduced; the providence (God giving the law of leprosy, the figure of sin, the priests to judge). The verse displays the LORD speaking concerning the law of leprosy.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the introduction — "the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron, saying," the law of leprosy (Henry, Gill); "the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron," the word (Gill, Ellicott); the command (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the speaking LORD. The verse marks the speaking: to Moses and Aaron. The verse displays the law: of leprosy. The verse displays the priests: to judge. The verse displays the figure: of sin. The speaking displays God giving the law of leprosy. The verse displays the LORD: speaking. The verse displays the law: of the plague. The verse displays the priests: discerning. The LORD speaking concerning the law of leprosy displays the divine command: the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron — God giving the law of leprosy, the great figure of sin, the priests to judge between clean and unclean. The verse displays the plague brought to the priest (v.2).
2:
"When a man shall have in the skin of his flesh a rising, a scab, or bright spot, and it be in the skin of his flesh like the plague of leprosy; then he shall be brought unto Aaron the priest, or unto one of his sons the priests:" The witnesses note the plague brought to the priest — the crux of leprosy as the figure of sin. On "when a man shall have... a rising, a scab, or bright spot... like the plague of leprosy; then he shall be brought unto Aaron the priest," interpreters expound the law. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the leprosy and the priest — "then he shall be brought unto Aaron the priest, or unto one of his sons the priests": when a suspicious mark appeared (a rising, scab, or bright spot like leprosy), the man was brought to the priest to be examined and judged; the witnesses note the significance — the priest the judge of the plague (not the physician, but the priest, for leprosy was a ceremonial and religious matter, a figure of sin and its defilement); the lesson — leprosy the great Old Testament figure of sin (the loathsome, defiling, spreading, separating disease, a picture of sin in the soul — its hidden beginnings, its corruption, its spreading defilement, its separation from God and his people; the leper unclean, cast out, requiring cleansing); the priest's office to discern and judge the plague (the figure of the discerning of sin, the bringing of the sinner to be judged); the figure — the man brought to the priest, as the sinner must be brought to Christ the great High Priest, who alone can discern and cleanse the leprosy of sin (the lepers brought to Christ, Matthew 8:2-3; Mark 1:40-42; Luke 17:12-14; the cleansing only he can give).

On "a rising, a scab, or bright spot," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the marks. Wesley and Keil note the various marks (the rising, scab, bright spot, the suspicious signs to be examined) — the figure (the marks of sin, to be discerned).

On the bringing, the grammatical-historical note the priest: brought unto the priest — the plague judged by the priest; the providence (leprosy the figure of sin, the priest the judge, the sinner brought to be discerned). The verse displays the plague brought to the priest.

The witnesses agree the substance: the man with a suspicious mark was brought to the priest to be judged (Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of leprosy as the figure of sin: the plague brought to the priest — leprosy the great figure of sin (the loathsome, defiling, spreading, separating disease, a picture of sin in the soul), and the priest the judge of the plague (the figure of the discerning of sin), the man brought to the priest as the sinner must be brought to Christ the great High Priest who alone can discern and cleanse the leprosy of sin (Matthew 8:2-3; Mark 1:40-42; Luke 17:12-14). The verse displays the leprosy-figure crux. The verse marks the plague: like leprosy. The verse displays the man: brought. The verse displays the priest: the judge. The verse displays the sin: figured. The bringing displays the sinner brought to be judged. The verse displays the leprosy: the figure of sin. The verse displays the priest: discerning. The verse displays the Christ: who cleanses. The plague brought to the priest displays the crux: brought unto the priest (Matthew 8:2-3; Mark 1:40-42; Luke 17:12-14) — leprosy the figure of sin, the priest the judge, the sinner brought as to Christ the great High Priest who alone can cleanse the leprosy of sin. The verse displays the marks of the plague (v.3).
3:
"And the priest shall look on the plague in the skin of the flesh: and when the hair in the plague is turned white, and the plague in sight be deeper than the skin of his flesh, it is a plague of leprosy: and the priest shall look on him, and pronounce him unclean." The witnesses note the marks of the plague — the crux of sin's deep and inward corruption. On "when the hair in the plague is turned white, and the plague in sight be deeper than the skin... it is a plague of leprosy," interpreters expound the marks. The reformed and the dominant reading note the signs — "the hair in the plague is turned white, and the plague... be deeper than the skin": two marks revealed true leprosy — the hair turned white (a sign of the deep, settled disease) and the plague deeper than the skin (the corruption reaching below the surface); the witnesses note the significance — the deep and inward corruption (the leprosy not a surface blemish but a deep-rooted disease, the corruption reaching below the skin, the marks of the settled plague); the lesson — sin's deep and inward nature (sin not a surface stain but a deep corruption of the heart, reaching below the surface, the inward defilement, "the whole head is sick, and the whole heart faint... from the sole of the foot even unto the head there is no soundness," Isaiah 1:5-6; the deceitfulness and depth of sin, Jeremiah 17:9); the priest's judgment (pronouncing unclean, the figure of the verdict of God's law on sin); the figure — the depth of the corruption that only the great High Priest can cleanse.

On "the priest shall look on the plague," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the examination. Wesley and Keil note the priest's careful examination (looking on the plague, judging by the marks) — the discernment (the careful judgment of the plague, the figure of the discerning of sin).

On "and pronounce him unclean," the grammatical-historical note the verdict: "the priest shall look on him, and pronounce him unclean" — the priest pronouncing unclean (the verdict on the plague); the figure (the verdict of God's law on sin, the sinner pronounced unclean, Romans 3:19); the providence (the unclean pronounced, the figure of sin judged). The verse displays the marks of the plague.

The witnesses agree the substance: the white hair and the depth below the skin marked true leprosy, and the priest pronounced unclean (Wesley, Keil, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of sin's deep and inward corruption: the marks of the plague — the hair turned white and the plague deeper than the skin, the marks of the deep, settled disease, a figure of sin's deep and inward nature (not a surface stain but a deep corruption of the heart, Isaiah 1:5-6; Jeremiah 17:9), the priest pronouncing unclean a figure of the verdict of God's law on sin (Romans 3:19). The verse displays the deep-corruption crux. The verse marks the hair: turned white. The verse displays the plague: deeper than the skin. The verse displays the corruption: inward. The verse displays the verdict: unclean. The marks display the deep, inward corruption of sin. The verse displays the leprosy: deep-rooted. The verse displays the sin: inward. The verse displays the verdict: of the law. The marks of the plague display the crux: the hair white, the plague deeper than the skin, pronounce him unclean (Isaiah 1:5-6; Jeremiah 17:9; Romans 3:19) — the deep and inward corruption of leprosy, a figure of sin's deep nature, the priest's verdict the figure of God's law on sin. The verse displays the shutting up of the doubtful (vv.4-6).
4:
"If the bright spot be white in the skin of his flesh, and in sight be not deeper than the skin, and the hair thereof be not turned white; then the priest shall shut up him that hath the plague seven days:" The very brief verse notes the shutting up of the doubtful for seven days. The grammatical voices note the shutting up. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "if the bright spot be white in the skin... and in sight be not deeper than the skin, and the hair thereof be not turned white; then the priest shall shut up him that hath the plague seven days" — if the marks were doubtful (the spot not deeper than the skin, the hair not white), the priest shut the man up for seven days, to watch the development; the doubtful case (the marks uncertain, not yet clearly leprosy); the shutting up (the man isolated, watched for seven days); the figure (the careful judgment, the time of watching; the not hasty condemning nor hasty clearing, the patient discernment).

On "in sight be not deeper than the skin, and the hair thereof be not turned white," the witnesses note the doubtful marks. Gill notes the doubtful marks (not deeper, not white, the case uncertain) — the discernment (the careful judgment, not hasty).

On "then the priest shall shut up him that hath the plague seven days," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the shutting up: "the priest shall shut up him that hath the plague seven days" — the man shut up, watched (the time of testing, the careful judgment); the figure (the patient discernment, the time of watching before the verdict); the providence (the doubtful watched, the verdict deferred, vv.5-6). The verse displays the shutting up of the doubtful for seven days.

5:
"And the priest shall look on him the seventh day: and, behold, if the plague in his sight be at a stay, and the plague spread not in the skin; then the priest shall shut him up seven days more:" The very brief verse notes the re-examination on the seventh day. The grammatical voices note the looking. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the priest shall look on him the seventh day: and, behold, if the plague in his sight be at a stay, and the plague spread not in the skin; then the priest shall shut him up seven days more" — on the seventh day the priest examined again; if the plague had stayed (not spread), the man was shut up another seven days; the re-examination (the priest looking again); the at a stay (the plague not spreading, the doubtful still uncertain); the seven days more (a second period of watching); the figure (the spreading the key sign of the plague; the careful, repeated judgment).

On "if the plague in his sight be at a stay, and the plague spread not," the witnesses note the spreading. Gill notes the plague not spreading (the key sign, the spreading marking the true leprosy) — the discernment (the spreading the test).

On "then the priest shall shut him up seven days more," the grammatical-historical note the second watching: "the priest shall shut him up seven days more" — the second period of watching (the careful, patient judgment); the figure (the repeated discernment, the watching of the spreading; sin known by its spreading, the small corruption that spreads, James 1:14-15); the providence (the doubtful watched again, the verdict still deferred, vv.6). The verse displays the re-examination on the seventh day.

6:
"And the priest shall look on him again the seventh day: and, behold, if the plague be somewhat dark, and the plague spread not in the skin, the priest shall pronounce him clean: it is but a scab: and he shall wash his clothes, and be clean." The very brief verse notes the clean pronouncement for the faded, non-spreading mark. The grammatical voices note the clean verdict. Ellicott and Gill note that "the priest shall look on him again the seventh day: and, behold, if the plague be somewhat dark, and the plague spread not in the skin, the priest shall pronounce him clean: it is but a scab" — on the second seventh day, if the plague had faded (somewhat dark, dimmer) and not spread, the priest pronounced the man clean — it was only a scab, not leprosy; the faded and not spreading (the marks of the harmless scab, not the spreading leprosy); the pronounce clean (the verdict of cleanness); the wash his clothes (the cleansing); the figure (the harmless blemish distinguished from the deadly plague; not every spot is leprosy; the careful discernment that does not condemn the clean).

On "if the plague be somewhat dark, and the plague spread not," the witnesses note the fading. Gill notes the plague faded and not spreading (the marks of cleanness, the scab) — the discernment (the harmless distinguished from the deadly).

The verse displays the clean pronouncement for the faded, non-spreading mark.

7:

The grammatical voice notes the spreading. Gill notes that "if the scab spread much abroad in the skin, after that he hath been seen of the priest for his cleansing, he shall be seen of the priest again" — if, after being pronounced clean, the scab spread, the man was to be seen by the priest again; the spreading after cleansing (the disease breaking out again, the apparent cleanness proving false); the seen again (the re-examination, the verdict reconsidered); the figure (the spreading the mark of the true plague; the sin that breaks out again, the false peace, the corruption that proves itself by spreading; the need of fresh examination).

The verse displays the spreading scab after the cleansing.

8:

9:

11:
"It is an old leprosy in the skin of his flesh, and the priest shall pronounce him unclean, and shall not shut him up: for he is unclean." The witnesses note the old leprosy pronounced unclean without shutting up. 12:

13:
"Then the priest shall consider: and, behold, if the leprosy have covered all his flesh, he shall pronounce him clean that hath the plague: it is all turned white: he is clean." The witnesses note the whole leprous pronounced clean — the crux of the paradox of confession. On "if the leprosy have covered all his flesh, he shall pronounce him clean... it is all turned white: he is clean," interpreters expound the paradox. The reformed and the dominant reading note the strange verdict — "if the leprosy have covered all his flesh, he shall pronounce him clean": strangely, when the leprosy covered the whole body and all turned white, the man was pronounced clean; the witnesses note the meaning — the disease, having come fully to the surface and turned all white (the corruption fully out, the crisis past, the disease no longer active and spreading but spent), was no longer ceremonially defiling; the lesson — the great spiritual paradox of confession (the figure that the one who is wholly covered, who acknowledges himself wholly corrupt and a sinner through and through, is pronounced clean — for he that confesses all his sin, hiding nothing, owning himself wholly leprous, is justified; "if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins," 1 John 1:9; the publican who said "God be merciful to me a sinner" went down justified, Luke 18:13-14; the contrast with the self-righteous who hides his spots); the figure — the sinner who owns his whole corruption is cleansed, while the one who hides a spot of seeming soundness (the raw flesh, vv.14) is unclean; the cleansing of the one who confesses all.

On "it is all turned white: he is clean," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the cleanness. Wesley and Keil note the all turned white pronounced clean (the disease come fully out, the man clean) — the figure (the one who owns himself wholly a sinner is cleansed, the confession of all sin); the lesson (the whole acknowledgment, the cleansing of the penitent).

On the verdict, the grammatical-historical note the paradox: the whole covered pronounced clean — the paradox of confession; the providence (the figure of the sinner who confesses all, justified, 1 John 1:9). The verse displays the whole leprous pronounced clean.

14:
"But when raw flesh appeareth in him, he shall be unclean." The very brief verse notes the raw flesh that makes unclean — the crux of the living corruption that defiles. On "when raw flesh appeareth in him, he shall be unclean," interpreters expound the contrast. The reformed and the dominant reading note the raw flesh — "when raw flesh appeareth in him, he shall be unclean": in contrast with the wholly-white leper pronounced clean (vv.13), if raw (living, open) flesh appeared, the man was unclean; the witnesses note the meaning — the raw flesh (the open, living sore, the active corruption, the disease still alive and spreading) defiled, whereas the all-white (the disease spent) was clean; the lesson — the great spiritual contrast (the figure that living, unmortified, unconfessed corruption defiles — the one spot of "raw flesh," the unacknowledged sin, the hidden living corruption, makes unclean, even where there is much seeming whiteness); the figure — the contrast between the one who confesses all (clean, vv.13) and the one who harbors a living, unconfessed sin (unclean); the danger of the one spot kept back, the secret sin not owned, the living corruption hidden (the leaven that leavens, the spot that defiles, the partial confession that is no confession); the cleansing of those who confess all and the defilement of those who keep back a living sin (Acts 5:1-11, Ananias; Joshua 7, Achan's hidden sin).

On "raw flesh," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the living sore. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the priest shall look upon it: and, behold, if the hair in the bright spot be turned white, and it be in sight deeper than the skin; it is a leprosy broken out of the burning... the priest shall pronounce him unclean" — if the hair turned white and the mark was deeper than the skin, it was leprosy broken out of the burn, and the priest pronounced unclean; the white hair and deeper than the skin (the marks of leprosy, vv.3); the broken out of the burning (the leprosy arising from the burn); the figure (the corruption breaking out of the old burn, as the boil; the deep, settled plague in the place of injury).

On "if the hair in the bright spot be turned white, and it be in sight deeper than the skin," the witnesses note the marks. Gill notes the marks of leprosy (white hair, deeper, vv.3) — the signs (the true plague).

The verse displays the burn's mark judged to be leprosy.

26:
"But if the priest look on it, and, behold, there be no white hair in the bright spot, and it be no lower than the other skin, but be somewhat dark; then the priest shall shut him up seven days:" The very brief verse notes the doubtful burn shut up for seven days. The grammatical voices note the doubtful marks. JFB and Gill note that "if the priest look on it, and, behold, there be no white hair in the bright spot, and it be no lower than the other skin, but be somewhat dark; then the priest shall shut him up seven days" — if the marks were doubtful (no white hair, not lower, somewhat dark), the priest shut the man up seven days; the doubtful marks (the signs uncertain, vv.21); the shut up seven days (the time of watching); the figure (the careful judgment of the doubtful case, as before).

On "there be no white hair in the bright spot, and it be no lower than the other skin, but be somewhat dark," the witnesses note the doubtful. Gill notes the doubtful marks (no white hair, not lower, dark, vv.21) — the discernment (the careful judgment).

On "then the priest shall shut him up seven days," the grammatical-historical note the watching: "the priest shall shut him up seven days" — the man watched (the time of testing); the figure (the patient discernment); the providence (the doubtful watched, the verdict deferred, vv.27-28). The verse displays the doubtful burn shut up for seven days.

27:
"And the priest shall look upon him the seventh day: and if it be spread much abroad in the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is the plague of leprosy." The very brief verse notes the spreading burn pronounced unclean. The grammatical voice notes the spreading. Gill notes that "the priest shall look upon him the seventh day: and if it be spread much abroad in the skin, then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is the plague of leprosy" — on the seventh day, if the mark had spread, the priest pronounced unclean — it was leprosy; the spreading (the decisive sign, vv.22); the pronounce unclean (the verdict); the figure (the spreading the proof of the plague, sin known by its spreading).

On "if it be spread much abroad in the skin," the witness notes the spreading. Gill notes the spreading the decisive sign (the plague proven, vv.22) — the figure (sin known by its spreading).

The verse displays the spreading burn pronounced unclean.

28:
"And if the bright spot stay in his place, and spread not in the skin, but it be somewhat dark; it is a rising of the burning, and the priest shall pronounce him clean: for it is an inflammation of the burning." The very brief verse notes the non-spreading burn pronounced clean. The grammatical voices note the verdict. Wesley and Gill note that "if the bright spot stay in his place, and spread not in the skin, but it be somewhat dark; it is a rising of the burning, and the priest shall pronounce him clean: for it is an inflammation of the burning" — if the spot stayed and did not spread, and was dim, it was only the rising (scar) of the burn, the inflammation, and the priest pronounced clean; the stay and spread not (the marks of the harmless scar, vv.23); the rising of the burning (the burn's scar, not leprosy); the pronounce clean (the verdict of cleanness); the figure (the harmless mark of the old injury distinguished from the deadly plague; not every mark is leprosy).

On "if the bright spot stay in his place, and spread not in the skin," the witnesses note the staying. Gill notes the spot not spreading (the harmless scar, vv.23) — the discernment (the harmless distinguished from the deadly).

The verse displays the non-spreading burn pronounced clean.

29:

30:
"Then the priest shall see the plague: and, behold, if it be in sight deeper than the skin; and there be in it a yellow thin hair; then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a dry scall, even a leprosy upon the head or beard." The very brief verse notes the scall judged to be leprosy. The grammatical voices note the marks. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the priest shall see the plague: and, behold, if it be in sight deeper than the skin; and there be in it a yellow thin hair; then the priest shall pronounce him unclean: it is a dry scall, even a leprosy upon the head or beard" — if the scall was deeper than the skin and had a yellow thin hair, the priest pronounced unclean — it was a dry scall, a leprosy of the head or beard; the deeper than the skin and yellow thin hair (the marks of the leprous scall, like the white hair and depth elsewhere, vv.3); the dry scall (the scaly leprosy of the hair); the pronounce unclean (the verdict); the figure (the leprosy of the head or beard, the corruption in the hair, the deep, settled plague).
